beginner.question: What does the display property do in CSS?
beginner.answer: The display property controls how an element is rendered in the document flow — whether it behaves as a block or inline element, whether it's a flex or grid container, whether it's visible or hidden, and how it interacts with other elements. It's one of the most fundamental CSS properties for layout.

beginner.question: What is the difference between display: block and display: inline?
beginner.answer: Block elements (display: block) take full available width, start on a new line, stack vertically, and respect width, height, margin, and padding on all sides. Inline elements (display: inline) flow horizontally like text, don't start new lines, ignore width/height, and only respect horizontal margins/padding. Examples: div is block, span is inline.

beginner.question: What does display: none do and how is it different from visibility: hidden?
beginner.answer: display: none completely removes the element from the document flow — it takes no space and is invisible. visibility: hidden hides the element visually but it still occupies its space in the layout (it's just invisible). With display: none, the element is not rendered at all. With visibility: hidden, the element's box is rendered but transparent.

beginner.question: What is display: inline-block?
beginner.answer: display: inline-block combines features of inline and block: the element flows inline (sits next to other elements) like inline, but it respects width, height, margin, and padding on ALL sides like block. This makes it useful for creating horizontal layouts (like navigation items) where you want element sizing without the elements stacking vertically.

beginner.question: How do you change a list item from vertical to horizontal using display?
beginner.answer: Set li { display: inline; } or li { display: inline-block; }. display: inline removes the bullet/number markers (unless you add them back with other CSS), while inline-block preserves the ability to set padding and margins for clickable areas. For more advanced horizontal layouts, use display: flex on the parent <ul>.

beginner.question: What is the default display value for a <div> element?
beginner.answer: The default display value for a <div> is block. This means it starts on a new line, takes up the full available width, and respects all box model properties. Other default block elements include: p, h1-h6, ul, ol, li, section, header, footer, nav, article, and main.

beginner.question: What is the default display value for a <span> element?
beginner.answer: The default display value for a <span> is inline. This means it flows horizontally within text, doesn't start a new line, and does not accept width, height, or vertical margin values. Other default inline elements include: a, strong, em, b, i, u, label, and img (though img is a replaced inline element with different sizing behavior).

beginner.question: How do you center an element horizontally using display and margin?
beginner.answer: For block elements with a set width: .center { display: block; width: 200px; margin: 0 auto; }. The auto margins distribute remaining space equally left and right. For inline or inline-block elements: text-align: center on the parent centers them. For flex items: display: flex; justify-content: center; on the parent.

beginner.question: What does display: flow-root do?
beginner.answer: display: flow-root creates a new Block Formatting Context (BFC) without using overflow or other side-effect-causing methods. It's the modern way to contain floats and prevent margin collapsing. Example: .clearfix { display: flow-root; } replaces the old clearfix hack. It ensures the container fully encloses floated children and prevents margins from collapsing.

beginner.question: Can you change the display property of any element?
beginner.answer: Yes, you can change the display value of any element in CSS. For example, you can make a <span> display: block or a <div> display: inline. However, changing display may affect the element's semantic meaning — for accessibility, it's better to choose the correct HTML element first. Some display changes (like making table rows inline) can produce unexpected results.

intermediate.question: How does changing display from inline to block affect the box model?
intermediate.answer: Changing an inline element to display: block makes it accept all box model properties: width, height, margin (all sides), and padding (all sides). It will also start on a new line and take full available width. For example, making a <span> display: block; width: 200px; margin: 10px; works perfectly, whereas inline would ignore width and vertical margins.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between display: flex and display: inline-flex?
intermediate.answer: display: flex creates a block-level flex container — it behaves like a block element (takes full width, starts on new line). display: inline-flex creates an inline-level flex container — it only takes the width needed for its content and flows inline with surrounding elements. Both create a flex formatting context for their children, but the container itself behaves differently.

intermediate.question: What is the inline-block whitespace gap and how do you fix it?
intermediate.answer: When inline-block elements are separated by whitespace in HTML (spaces, newlines), the whitespace renders as a visible gap between elements (typically 4px). Fixes include: (1) remove whitespace in HTML (elements on same line); (2) font-size: 0 on parent + reset on children; (3) negative margin; (4) display: flex instead (no gap). The flex solution is the most robust.

intermediate.question: What does display: contents do?
intermediate.answer: display: contents makes the element's box disappear from the layout — the element itself is not rendered, but its children are rendered as if they were children of the element's parent. The element's background, padding, border, and margin are all ignored. This is useful for wrapping elements that are needed for semantics or JavaScript but interfere with CSS layout (e.g., in flex or grid layouts where the wrapper breaks the layout).

intermediate.question: How do table display values (table, table-row, table-cell) work for non-table HTML?
intermediate.answer: Use display: table on a container, display: table-row on rows, display: table-cell on cells. This makes non-table elements behave like a table layout — cells automatically align in columns, adjacent rows share column widths, and vertical-align: middle works. Example: .container { display: table; width: 100%; } .row { display: table-row; } .cell { display: table-cell; vertical-align: middle; }. This was a common layout technique before flexbox/grid.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between display: none and visibility: collapse?
intermediate.answer: visibility: collapse is primarily for table elements — it hides a table row/column but the space is removed (similar to display: none for tables). On non-table elements, visibility: collapse behaves the same as visibility: hidden. Display: none removes the element entirely. visibility: collapse on table rows removes the row's space, which is useful for dynamically hiding table rows without layout disruption.

intermediate.question: How does the display property interact with CSS animations and transitions?
intermediate.answer: display cannot be animated or transitioned directly (no intermediate states between display: block and display: none). To animate an element's appearance/disappearance: use opacity + visibility + height for the animation, then set display: none at the end using animation-fill-mode. Or use the `display` property in a @keyframes animation at the 0% and 100% marks only (allowed in modern browsers as discrete animation).

intermediate.question: What does display: run-in do and why is it rarely used?
intermediate.answer: display: run-in makes an element behave as inline if it's followed by a block element (the run-in element 'runs into' the following block). Otherwise it behaves as block. In theory, it could be used for heading + paragraph combinations where the heading appears inline with the first paragraph. In practice, browser support for run-in is poor and inconsistent (removed from CSS Grid module, deprecated in some contexts), so it's not used in production.

intermediate.question: How do you use display to hide an element while keeping it accessible to screen readers?
intermediate.answer: display: none hides from all users including screen readers. To visually hide but keep accessible: use a clip pattern: .sr-only { position: absolute; width: 1px; height: 1px; padding: 0; margin: -1px; overflow: hidden; clip: rect(0, 0, 0, 0); white-space: nowrap; border: 0; }. This visually hides content while keeping it in the accessibility tree for screen readers.

intermediate.question: What is the default display value for and why does it behave differently for width/height?
intermediate.answer: The default display for is inline (or inline-block in some browsers). However, as a replaced element, it behaves differently from non-replaced inline elements: width and height ARE respected (because the image has intrinsic dimensions). This is unique to replaced elements — they're inline-level but accept width/height. The default vertical alignment is baseline, which creates a small gap below images.

advanced.question: Explain the complete block formatting context (BFC) and which display values create one.
advanced.answer: A BFC is a region where block-level boxes are laid out according to block layout rules, isolated from outside floats and margins. BFCs are created by: display: flow-root, display: flex/inline-flex, display: grid/inline-grid, display: table/inline-table, display: table-cell, display: table-caption; also by float: left/right, position: absolute/fixed, overflow: hidden/scroll/auto (not visible). BFCs prevent margin collapsing between children and the parent, contain floats, and prevent elements from overlapping floats outside the BFC.

advanced.question: How does the CSS display property interact with the element's role in the accessibility tree?
advanced.answer: The display property affects whether elements appear in the accessibility tree. display: none removes the element from the accessibility tree (screen readers can't see it). display: contents removes the element's own accessibility role but its children remain. display: table-* maintains table semantics. Changing display between block/inline/flex/grid doesn't change the semantic role assigned by HTML. However, some ARIA roles require specific display values to function correctly.

advanced.question: How do subgrid and masonry display values relate to display: grid?
advanced.answer: display: subgrid (not a standalone display value but a grid-template-columns/rows value) allows a nested grid to inherit track definitions from its parent grid. It's specified as grid-template-columns: subgrid; on a grid item. display: masonry (proposed) would create a masonry/pinterest-style layout. As of 2024, subgrid is supported in major browsers, while masonry is only in Firefox behind a flag. Neither is a new display value — they extend grid behavior within the existing display: grid model.

advanced.question: How do CSS containment and display interact for performance optimization?
advanced.answer: display and containment work together: display: none implies contain: all (the element and its subtree are not rendered). contain: layout style paint on visible elements creates boundaries that optimize rendering. Without a display value that creates a formatting context, contain may not work as expected. display: flow-root + contain: layout is a powerful combination for isolating components — the flow-root creates a BFC, and contain prevents the component from needing repaints when its content changes.

advanced.question: Explain the interaction between display and position: absolute/fixed behavior.
advanced.answer: When an element has position: absolute or position: fixed, the computed display value changes to a 'blockified' display — inline becomes block, inline-flex becomes flex, inline-grid becomes grid, inline-table becomes table, etc. The element becomes a block-level container. This is why you can set width/height on an absolutely positioned <span> even though it's normally inline — the position overrides the display to block.

advanced.question: How does the display property interact with the float and clear properties?
advanced.answer: When an element is floated, its computed display value changes: block is used for most elements (flex inline-block becomes block). The float property only works on elements that can be block-level. If you float an inline element, it becomes block. Similarly, clear only works on block-level elements. This is why floating converts inline to block behavior. Modern layouts avoid float for layout, using flexbox/grid instead.

advanced.question: What is the difference between display: list-item and display: block on <li> elements?
advanced.answer: display: list-item (the default for <li>) generates a principal block box plus a marker box (for the bullet/number). display: block on <li> removes the marker box entirely — no bullet or number is displayed, and list-style properties have no effect. The element still behaves as a block but without the list-specific formatting. Both maintain the <li> semantic role in the accessibility tree.

advanced.question: How does CSS display property inheritance work and why is it not inherited by default?
advanced.answer: display is NOT inherited — each element has its own initial display value based on the HTML element type (block for div, inline for span, etc.). Even though a child of a flex container becomes a flex item, its display property value doesn't change — it just participates in flex layout. If display were inherited, all elements inside a flex container would become block-level, breaking inline elements inside paragraphs.

advanced.question: How do you use display: inline-grid for inline grid layouts that don't break text flow?
advanced.answer: display: inline-grid creates a grid that flows inline with surrounding content, similar to how inline-block works. This is useful for small grid elements within text (like inline calendars, mini dashboards, or icon grids): .inline-grid { display: inline-grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 1fr); gap: 2px; vertical-align: middle; }. The grid only takes as much width as its content, allowing it to sit within a line of text.

advanced.question: What happens to the display value when an element has both display and !important in inline styles?
advanced.answer: An inline style with !important (style="display: block !important;") overrides all other declarations including stylesheet rules with !important. However, the cascade still follows the usual rules — if two !important declarations conflict, specificity and source order determine the winner. The display value ultimately determines the element's formatting context, which affects how descendants are laid out. Changing from display: flex to display: block changes the formatting context for children from flex to block.
